notes: Founded 1984 by Len Bosack and Sandy Lerner at Stanford; $4B revenue trailing four quarters at time of report; $40B market cap in 1997; became largest networking company in the world; peak market cap ~$555B in March 2000 during dot-com bubble; still active and dominant as of 2026 (NASDAQ: CSCO).

Leading high-end ATM/frame relay switch supplier; acquired by Cisco for $4.67B in June 1996 (Cisco's largest acquisition to date at that point); StrataCom BPX switch and Stratm Technology became key to Cisco's ISP and WAN switching strategy; integrated into Cisco's service provider product line.

Referenced in report as a networking vendor adding routing intelligence to switches; formed by merger of Synoptics and Wellfleet (1994); acquired by Nortel Networks for $9.1B (1998); Nortel ultimately failed; Bay Networks assets dispersed.
Referenced in report as competitor adding routing intelligence to switches; see separate Cabletron profile study.
Referenced as announcing IP switching devices; mid-tier networking vendor; acquired by HP for $2.7B (2010); HP spun off enterprise division as HPE; 3Com brand discontinued.
Referenced as announcing IP switching devices; WAN/ATM networking vendor; acquired by Lucent Technologies for $20B (1999); became part of Lucent then Alcatel-Lucent.
